• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

RNA-seq Analysis: Dexamethasone Treatment in Airway Cells

Despoina Eleftheriadou

2026-06-14

Introduction

In this analysis/exercise, we analyse an RNA-seq experiment where human airway smooth muscle cells were treated with dexamethasone (a glucocorticoid steroid used to treat asthma) or left untreated.

The goal is to find genes that change their expression in response to the drug.

The data comes from the “airway” Bioconductor package (Himes et al. 2014, PLoS ONE). It contains read counts for ~64000 genes across 8 samples (4 treated, 4 untreated).

Part 1: The Data

Install and load packages

library(airway)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(DESeq2)
library(clusterProfiler)
library(org.Hs.eg.db)
library(AnnotationDbi)

Load the dataset

data("airway")
airway
## class: RangedSummarizedExperiment 
## dim: 63677 8 
## metadata(1): ''
## assays(1): counts
## rownames(63677): ENSG00000000003 ENSG00000000005 ... ENSG00000273492
##   ENSG00000273493
## rowData names(10): gene_id gene_name ... seq_coord_system symbol
## colnames(8): SRR1039508 SRR1039509 ... SRR1039520 SRR1039521
## colData names(9): SampleName cell ... Sample BioSample

What is this object?

airway is a RangedSummarizedExperiment.

It is a standard container in Bioconductor that stores the count matrix (how many reads mapped to each gene in each sample), sample information (which sample is treated, which cell line, etc.) and gene coordinates (where each gene sits on the genome) together.

Sample information (metadata)

airway contains information about the samples.

It captures the experimental design: which sample belongs to which condition.

We can extract the table that describes each sample with colData. colData stands for column data — because each column of the count matrix is one sample, and this function retrieves the table that describes those columns.

design <- as.data.frame(colData(airway))
design
##            SampleName    cell   dex albut        Run avgLength Experiment
## SRR1039508 GSM1275862  N61311 untrt untrt SRR1039508       126  SRX384345
## SRR1039509 GSM1275863  N61311   trt untrt SRR1039509       126  SRX384346
## SRR1039512 GSM1275866 N052611 untrt untrt SRR1039512       126  SRX384349
## SRR1039513 GSM1275867 N052611   trt untrt SRR1039513        87  SRX384350
## SRR1039516 GSM1275870 N080611 untrt untrt SRR1039516       120  SRX384353
## SRR1039517 GSM1275871 N080611   trt untrt SRR1039517       126  SRX384354
## SRR1039520 GSM1275874 N061011 untrt untrt SRR1039520       101  SRX384357
## SRR1039521 GSM1275875 N061011   trt untrt SRR1039521        98  SRX384358
##               Sample    BioSample
## SRR1039508 SRS508568 SAMN02422669
## SRR1039509 SRS508567 SAMN02422675
## SRR1039512 SRS508571 SAMN02422678
## SRR1039513 SRS508572 SAMN02422670
## SRR1039516 SRS508575 SAMN02422682
## SRR1039517 SRS508576 SAMN02422673
## SRR1039520 SRS508579 SAMN02422683
## SRR1039521 SRS508580 SAMN02422677

We can see how many and which samples are treated and which are untreated:

treated <- design %>% filter(dex == "trt") %>% dplyr::select(dex)
treated
##            dex
## SRR1039509 trt
## SRR1039513 trt
## SRR1039517 trt
## SRR1039521 trt
# how many treated (percentage)
nrow(treated) / nrow(design) * 100
## [1] 50
untreated <- design %>% filter(dex == "untrt") %>% dplyr::select(dex)
untreated
##              dex
## SRR1039508 untrt
## SRR1039512 untrt
## SRR1039516 untrt
## SRR1039520 untrt
# how many untreated (percentage)
nrow(untreated) / nrow(design) * 100
## [1] 50

The count matrix

assay() is a function from the SummarizedExperiment package. It extracts the numerical data stored inside a SummarizedExperiment object. The reason it exists is that a SummarizedExperiment can store multiple matrices for the same experiment — for example:

  • “counts” — the raw read counts
  • “normCounts” — normalised counts
  • “logCounts” — log-transformed counts

Check what matrices are available in an object with:

assayNames(airway)
## [1] "counts"

assay(airway, "counts") means: “from the airway object, give me specifically the matrix called counts”

counts_raw <- assay(airway, "counts")

head(counts_raw)
##                 SRR1039508 SRR1039509 SRR1039512 SRR1039513 SRR1039516
## ENSG00000000003        679        448        873        408       1138
## ENSG00000000005          0          0          0          0          0
## ENSG00000000419        467        515        621        365        587
## ENSG00000000457        260        211        263        164        245
## ENSG00000000460         60         55         40         35         78
## ENSG00000000938          0          0          2          0          1
##                 SRR1039517 SRR1039520 SRR1039521
## ENSG00000000003       1047        770        572
## ENSG00000000005          0          0          0
## ENSG00000000419        799        417        508
## ENSG00000000457        331        233        229
## ENSG00000000460         63         76         60
## ENSG00000000938          0          0          0
dim(counts_raw)
## [1] 63677     8

There are 63677 transcripts and 8 samples. In the counts_raw matrix each row is one transcript and each column is one sample. The number in each cell is how many reads mapped to that transcript in that sample (not normalized).

Part 2: Exploring the Counts

Library sizes

Let’s check the library size for each sample:

library_sizes <- colSums(counts_raw)
library_sizes
## SRR1039508 SRR1039509 SRR1039512 SRR1039513 SRR1039516 SRR1039517 SRR1039520 
##   20637971   18809481   25348649   15163415   24448408   30818215   19126151 
## SRR1039521 
##   21164133
library_sizes_tibble <- tibble(
  sample      = names(library_sizes),
  total_reads = library_sizes
) %>%
  left_join(
    design %>% rownames_to_column("sample") %>% 
      dplyr::select(sample, dex), 
    by = "sample") %>%
  rename(condition = dex)
head(library_sizes_tibble)
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   sample     total_reads condition
##   <chr>            <dbl> <fct>    
## 1 SRR1039508    20637971 untrt    
## 2 SRR1039509    18809481 trt      
## 3 SRR1039512    25348649 untrt    
## 4 SRR1039513    15163415 trt      
## 5 SRR1039516    24448408 untrt    
## 6 SRR1039517    30818215 trt

Each sample has a different total number of mapped reads (library size).

ggplot(library_sizes_tibble, aes(x = sample, y = total_reads, fill = condition)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(title = "Library sizes per sample",
       x     = "Sample",
       y     = "Total mapped reads",
       fill  = "Condition") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

We see that the library sizes are different for each sample, so a normalization step is necessary before comparing the expression of the same gene across different samples. The normalized reads will be in CPM (counts per million).

Also we count the genes with zero counts across all samples and remove those before analysis, because they carry no information and it is computationally more expensive and biologically not informative to deal with zero-count genes.

n_zero <- sum(rowSums(counts_raw) == 0)
cat("Genes with zero counts across all samples:", n_zero, "\n")
## Genes with zero counts across all samples: 30208
cat("Total genes:", nrow(counts_raw), "\n")
## Total genes: 63677
counts_filtered <- counts_raw[rowSums(counts_raw) > 0, ]
cat("Genes remaining after filtering:", nrow(counts_filtered), "\n")
## Genes remaining after filtering: 33469

Part 3: Normalization CPM

Raw counts cannot be compared directly between samples because of different library sizes. We normalize to CPM (Counts Per Million):

\[CPM = \frac{\text{raw count}}{\text{library size}} \times 10^6\]

This makes counts comparable across samples while keeping the relative differences between genes the same within a sample.

library_sizes_filtered <- colSums(counts_filtered)

cpm <- sweep(counts_filtered, 2, library_sizes_filtered, FUN = "/") * 1e6

# Check: each column should now sum to 1,000,000
colSums(cpm)
## SRR1039508 SRR1039509 SRR1039512 SRR1039513 SRR1039516 SRR1039517 SRR1039520 
##      1e+06      1e+06      1e+06      1e+06      1e+06      1e+06      1e+06 
## SRR1039521 
##      1e+06

sweep() applies an operation between a matrix and a vector, row by row or column by column. Breaking down the arguments:

  • counts_filtered — the matrix to operate on
  • 2 — apply along columns (1 would mean rows)
  • library_sizes_filtered — the vector to use in the operation
  • FUN = "/" — divide

Distribution of CPM values

log_cpm <- log2(cpm + 1)

log_cpm %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "sample", values_to = "log2_cpm") %>%
  left_join(
    design %>%
      rownames_to_column("sample") %>%
      dplyr::select(sample, dex),
    by = "sample"
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = log2_cpm, colour = dex, group = sample)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(title  = "Distribution of log2(CPM + 1) per sample",
       x      = "log2(CPM + 1)",
       y      = "Density",
       colour = "Sample") +
  theme_minimal()

The CPM distribution is a quality control step to check if after normalization all samples have a similar distribution of expression values, so the normalization step worked.

The density curves show a similar distribution shape across all samples — a large peak near zero (lowly expressed genes) and a broader shoulder of moderately to highly expressed genes. Treated and untreated samples overlap well, confirming there is no global systematic difference between conditions and that the data is suitable for differential expression analysis.

Part 4: PCA and Clustering

Before comparing conditions, we check whether the samples behave as expected. Check if treated samples cluster together and separately from untreated.

PCA (Principal Component Analysis)

PCA reduces the ~60,000 gene dimensions down to a few components that capture the most variation in the data. We then plot the first two components (PC1, PC2) to see how samples relate to each other.

pca_result <- prcomp(t(log_cpm))

percent_variance <- summary(pca_result)$importance["Proportion of Variance", ] * 100
percent_variance
##    PC1    PC2    PC3    PC4    PC5    PC6    PC7    PC8 
## 37.165 23.436 17.363 12.111  4.189  3.168  2.568  0.000
pca_plot <- as_tibble(pca_result$x, rownames = "sample") %>%
  mutate(condition = design$dex,
         cell_line = design$cell)

ggplot(pca_plot, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, colour = condition, label = sample, shape = cell_line)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_text_repel() +
  labs(x = paste0("PC1: ", round(percent_variance[1], 1), "%"),
       y = paste0("PC2: ", round(percent_variance[2], 1), "%")) +
  theme_minimal()

The samples are separated based on conditions across PC1. All 4 untreated samples are on the left, all 4 treated are on the right. This means the biggest source of variation in the data is the dexamethasone treatment, which is exactly what we want to see. PC1 captures 37.2% of the total variance.

PC2 captures 23.4% of variance and reflects the cell line effect. SRR1039516 (untreated) and SRR1039517 (treated) are both high on PC2 compared to the other 6 samples — those two samples come from a different cell line.

Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram

A dendrogram groups samples by similarity. Samples that are closer together (shorter branch length) are more similar in their overall expression profile.

sample_dists <- dist(t(log_cpm), method = "euclidean")
hc <- hclust(sample_dists, method = "average")

plot(hc,
     main = "Hierarchical clustering of samples",
     xlab = "",
     sub  = "",
     ylab = "Euclidean distance")

The dendrogram shows two clearly separated branches. All four untreated samples cluster together on the left, and all four treated samples cluster together on the right. The height at which the two groups merge is greater than the height within each group, confirming that the treatment effect is the dominant source of variation. No outlier samples are detected. This result is consistent with the PCA and confirms the experiment is of good quality.

Part 5: Differential Expression with DESeq2

So far we have normalized our data and confirmed the quality. Now we do the actual analysis: finding genes that are significantly differentially expressed between treated and untreated samples.

We use DESeq2, the standard tool for this. DESeq2 accounts for biological variability across replicates and computes proper statistics — a p-value and an adjusted p-value for every gene.

Set up the DESeq2 object

# DESeq2 works directly from the raw counts (NOT CPM — it does its own normalisation)

# Model without cell line (for comparison)
dds0 <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts_raw,
  colData   = design,
  design    = ~ dex
)

# Model with cell line (better — removes cell line noise)
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts_raw,
  colData   = design,
  design    = ~ cell + dex
)

dds
## class: DESeqDataSet 
## dim: 63677 8 
## metadata(1): version
## assays(1): counts
## rownames(63677): ENSG00000000003 ENSG00000000005 ... ENSG00000273492
##   ENSG00000273493
## rowData names(0):
## colnames(8): SRR1039508 SRR1039509 ... SRR1039520 SRR1039521
## colData names(9): SampleName cell ... Sample BioSample

Run DESeq2

dds0 <- DESeq(dds0)
dds  <- DESeq(dds)

Extract the results

res0 <- results(dds0, contrast = c("dex", "trt", "untrt"), lfcThreshold = 0.25, alpha = 0.05)
res  <- results(dds,  contrast = c("dex", "trt", "untrt"), lfcThreshold = 0.25, alpha = 0.05)

summary(res0)
## 
## out of 33469 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0.25 (up)    : 630, 1.9%
## LFC < -0.25 (down) : 426, 1.3%
## outliers [1]       : 51, 0.15%
## low counts [2]     : 12979, 39%
## (mean count < 2)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
summary(res)
## 
## out of 33469 with nonzero total read count
## adjusted p-value < 0.05
## LFC > 0.25 (up)    : 951, 2.8%
## LFC < -0.25 (down) : 763, 2.3%
## outliers [1]       : 0, 0%
## low counts [2]     : 14215, 42%
## (mean count < 3)
## [1] see 'cooksCutoff' argument of ?results
## [2] see 'independentFiltering' argument of ?results
# Build the main results table from res (cell + dex model)
results_table <- as.data.frame(res) %>%
  rownames_to_column("gene_id") %>%
  as_tibble()

The results table has these key columns:

  • log2FoldChange — how much the gene changed (positive = up in treated)
  • pvalue — raw p-value
  • padj — adjusted p-value (corrected for multiple testing across ~60k genes)

Compared to the model without cell line (~ dex only), including the cell line in the design (~ cell + dex) increased the number of significant genes because it removes the cell line noise from the residuals, giving DESeq2 more statistical power. We use padj instead of pvalue because we are testing tens of thousands of genes simultaneously — DESeq2 corrects for multiple testing using the Benjamini-Hochberg method, which controls the false discovery rate (FDR).

Part 6: Diagnostics

Before looking at results, we check that DESeq2’s statistical model fit the data correctly. These three plots are standard checks in any DESeq2 analysis.

Dispersion plot

DESeq2 estimates the dispersion (variability) of each gene across samples. It first estimates a per-gene dispersion, then shrinks these estimates towards a fitted trend to improve reliability — especially for genes with low counts.

plotDispEsts(dds)

The plot looks healthy. The red trend line decreases smoothly from left to right, confirming that low-expressed genes are more variable than highly expressed ones — a well-known property of RNA-seq count data. The blue (final) estimates cluster tightly around the trend, meaning DESeq2’s shrinkage worked well. The model is appropriate for this data.

P-value histogram

A histogram of raw p-values tells us whether the statistical test behaved correctly.

# Use results without LFC threshold for this diagnostic

# model with cell line
res1 <- results(dds)

results_table1 <- as.data.frame(res1) %>%
  rownames_to_column("gene_id") %>%
  as_tibble()

# model without cell line
results_table0 <- as.data.frame(results(dds0)) %>%
  rownames_to_column("gene_id") %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(model = "~ dex")

results_table1 <- results_table1 %>%
  mutate(model = "~ cell + dex")

bind_rows(results_table0, results_table1) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = pvalue)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.05, fill = "hotpink", color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ model) +
  labs(title = "P-value distribution comparison",
       x     = "Raw p-value",
       y     = "Frequency") +
  theme_minimal()

The spike near 0 is much taller compared to the model without cell line — including cell in the design removed noise and gave DESeq2 more power to detect true differences. The flat middle region (0.1–0.7) represents null genes, uniformly distributed as expected. The residual spike near 0.75–1.0 is caused by DESeq2’s independent filtering: genes that barely pass the count threshold get very high p-values because they don’t have enough counts for reliable testing. This is expected behaviour for this dataset and not a sign of a model problem.

MA plot

DESeq2’s built-in MA plot shows log2 fold change (y-axis) vs mean expression (x-axis). Points are coloured by significance. DESeq2 applies fold change shrinkage to reduce noise from low-count genes — their fold changes are pulled towards zero.

plotMA(res, ylim = c(-5, 5),
       main = "MA plot: Treated vs Untreated")

The plot shows significant genes (blue) spread both above and below 0, confirming dexamethasone both activates and represses genes. The fan shape on the left is typical — low-count genes have noisier fold change estimates. At higher expression levels (right side), the fold changes tighten around 0 for non-significant genes, as expected.

Part 7: Visualisation

Volcano plot

A volcano plot shows statistical significance (−log10 adjusted p-value) on the y-axis and fold change on the x-axis. Genes that are both highly significant and strongly changed appear in the top corners.

results_table %>%
  filter(!is.na(padj)) %>%
  mutate(
    direction = case_when(
      padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange >  1 ~ "Up",
      padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange < -1 ~ "Down",
      TRUE                               ~ "NS"
    ),
    label = ifelse(padj < 1e-10 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 2, gene_id, NA)
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(padj), colour = direction)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = label), size = 3, colour = "black", max.overlaps = 15) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1, 1), linetype = "dashed", colour = "black") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", colour = "black") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Up" = "red", "Down" = "blue", "NS" = "grey")) +
  labs(title  = "Volcano plot: Treated vs Untreated",
       x      = "log2 Fold Change",
       y      = "-log10(adjusted p-value)",
       colour = "Direction") +
  theme_minimal()

Top significant genes

top_up <- results_table %>%
  filter(padj < 0.05, log2FoldChange > 1) %>%
  arrange(desc(log2FoldChange)) %>%
  head(20)

top_up %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(gene_id, log2FoldChange), y = log2FoldChange)) +
  geom_col(fill = "aquamarine3") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 20 up-regulated genes (Treated vs Untreated)",
       x     = "Gene",
       y     = "log2 Fold Change") +
  theme_minimal()

top_down <- results_table %>%
  filter(padj < 0.05, log2FoldChange < -1) %>%
  arrange(log2FoldChange) %>%
  head(20)

top_down %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(gene_id, abs(log2FoldChange)), y = abs(log2FoldChange))) +
  geom_col(fill = "darkorange") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 20 down-regulated genes (Treated vs Untreated)",
       x     = "Gene",
       y     = "|log2 Fold Change|") +
  theme_minimal()

Part 8: GO Term Enrichment Analysis

Instead of looking up individual genes, we can ask a bigger question: which biological processes are enriched among our differentially expressed genes?

GO (Gene Ontology) enrichment analysis tests whether genes from specific biological processes appear more often in our DE gene list than expected by chance. We use the clusterProfiler package.

Run GO enrichment on up-regulated genes

sig_up <- results_table %>%
  filter(padj < 0.05, log2FoldChange > 0.25) %>%
  pull(gene_id)

ego_up <- enrichGO(
  gene          = sig_up,
  OrgDb         = org.Hs.eg.db,
  keyType       = "ENSEMBL",
  ont           = "BP",
  pAdjustMethod = "BH",
  pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
  qvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
  readable      = TRUE
)

head(as.data.frame(ego_up)[, c("Description", "GeneRatio", "pvalue", "p.adjust")])
##                                              Description GeneRatio       pvalue
## GO:0031589                       cell-substrate adhesion    57/803 3.418763e-17
## GO:0010810         regulation of cell-substrate adhesion    37/803 1.303197e-12
## GO:0071375 cellular response to peptide hormone stimulus    44/803 1.002301e-11
## GO:0030198             extracellular matrix organization    43/803 2.050342e-10
## GO:0043062          extracellular structure organization    43/803 2.226351e-10
## GO:0007160                          cell-matrix adhesion    36/803 2.575595e-10
##                p.adjust
## GO:0031589 1.736048e-13
## GO:0010810 3.308818e-09
## GO:0071375 1.696561e-08
## GO:0030198 1.902398e-07
## GO:0043062 1.902398e-07
## GO:0007160 1.902398e-07

Visualise — dotplot

dotplot(ego_up, showCategory = 15,
        title = "GO Biological Processes — Up-regulated genes",
        font.size = 8)

The top enriched GO terms make strong biological sense for dexamethasone.

Response to peptide hormone and cellular response to peptide hormone stimulus reflect that dexamethasone acts as a steroid hormone, triggering hormone response pathways.

Cell-substrate adhesion and regulation of actin cytoskeleton are consistent with glucocorticoids remodelling the cytoskeleton and altering cell attachment.

Response to oxidative stress reflects dexamethasone’s antioxidant properties.

Response to insulin also appears — glucocorticoids are known to interfere with insulin signalling, which explains why long-term steroid use can cause diabetes.

Run GO enrichment on down-regulated genes

sig_down <- results_table %>%
  filter(padj < 0.05, log2FoldChange < -0.25) %>%
  pull(gene_id)

ego_down <- enrichGO(
  gene          = sig_down,
  OrgDb         = org.Hs.eg.db,
  keyType       = "ENSEMBL",
  ont           = "BP",
  pAdjustMethod = "BH",
  pvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
  qvalueCutoff  = 0.05,
  readable      = TRUE
)

head(as.data.frame(ego_down)[, c("Description", "GeneRatio", "pvalue", "p.adjust")])
##                                                  Description GeneRatio
## GO:0007409                                      axonogenesis    45/670
## GO:0007411                                     axon guidance    30/670
## GO:0097485                        neuron projection guidance    30/670
## GO:0035265                                      organ growth    26/670
## GO:0048839                             inner ear development    27/670
## GO:0051962 positive regulation of nervous system development    33/670
##                  pvalue     p.adjust
## GO:0007409 2.626236e-10 9.812645e-07
## GO:0007411 5.575351e-10 9.812645e-07
## GO:0097485 6.131625e-10 9.812645e-07
## GO:0035265 1.421846e-09 1.699640e-06
## GO:0048839 1.770090e-09 1.699640e-06
## GO:0051962 2.548473e-09 1.792694e-06
dotplot(ego_down, showCategory = 15,
        title = "GO Biological Processes — Down-regulated genes",
        font.size = 8)

The down-regulated terms are more surprising at first glance — axonogenesis, axon guidance, neuron projection guidance, and positive regulation of nervous system development suggest that dexamethasone suppresses neuronal-related gene programmes.

This is consistent with known glucocorticoid effects on the nervous system: long-term steroid use can affect neurodevelopment and neural plasticity.

Monocyte differentiation and regulation of monocyte differentiation are particularly relevant — dexamethasone is an anti-inflammatory drug and suppressing monocyte (immune cell) differentiation is one of its key mechanisms of action.

DNA damage response via p53 being down-regulated is also notable, as glucocorticoids can modulate p53 activity.

Fisher’s exact test on the top GO term

The dotplot shows enrichment visually, but we can also test it statistically with a Fisher’s exact test. We focus on the most enriched GO term: response to peptide hormone (GO:0043434).

What are we testing? We want to know whether genes annotated to this GO term appear among our differentially expressed genes more often than expected by chance.

Null hypothesis (H₀): Being differentially expressed and belonging to the GO term “response to peptide hormone” are independent. In other words, knowing a gene is in this GO term tells you nothing about whether it is DE.

Alternative hypothesis (H₁): DE genes are enriched for this GO term — they belong to it at a higher rate than non-DE genes.

We use Fisher’s exact test rather than chi-square because some cell counts are small — Fisher’s does not rely on a large-sample approximation and is the standard choice in this situation. It is also what enrichGO() uses internally.

We build a 2×2 contingency table for every gene tested:

In GO term Not in GO term
DE a b
Not DE c d

If H₀ is true, the proportion a/(a+b) should equal c/(c+d).

go_genes <- AnnotationDbi::select(
  org.Hs.eg.db,
  keys    = "GO:0043434",
  columns = "ENSEMBL",
  keytype = "GO"
)$ENSEMBL

results_annotated <- results_table %>%
  filter(!is.na(padj)) %>%
  mutate(
    is_de = padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 0.25,
    in_go = gene_id %in% go_genes
  )

cont_table <- table(
  DE    = results_annotated$is_de,
  In_GO = results_annotated$in_go
)

print(cont_table)
##        In_GO
## DE      FALSE  TRUE
##   FALSE 17511    29
##   TRUE   1706     8
fisher_result <- fisher.test(cont_table)
fisher_result
## 
##  Fisher's Exact Test for Count Data
## 
## data:  cont_table
## p-value = 0.01446
## alternative hypothesis: true odds ratio is not equal to 1
## 95 percent confidence interval:
##  1.116649 6.360258
## sample estimates:
## odds ratio 
##   2.831257
prop_de     <- cont_table["TRUE",  "TRUE"] / sum(cont_table["TRUE",  ])
prop_non_de <- cont_table["FALSE", "TRUE"] / sum(cont_table["FALSE", ])

cat("Proportion of DE genes in GO term:     ", round(prop_de, 4), "\n")
## Proportion of DE genes in GO term:      0.0047
cat("Proportion of non-DE genes in GO term: ", round(prop_non_de, 4), "\n")
## Proportion of non-DE genes in GO term:  0.0017

The contingency table shows 8 DE genes and 29 non-DE genes belonging to this GO term.

Fisher’s exact test gives p-value = 0.014 and odds ratio = 2.83.

Since p < 0.05 we reject H₀ — DE genes and GO term membership are not independent.

DE genes are 2.83× more likely to be annotated to “response to peptide hormone” than non-DE genes.

However, the 95% confidence interval just crosses 1, indicating the result is borderline significant. The small number of genes in this GO term limits statistical power, and the result should be interpreted as supporting rather than confirming the enrichment already identified by enrichGO().
